content.problemStatement:
Deletion from any position other than front is a little different.

For example, we have 1 -> 2 -> 3 and we want to remove 2.

For that, we have to point the next of 1 to 3 and delete 2.

Video Explanation
TaskComplete the function **deleteNode** to delete an element from any position.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers n and x, where n is the number of elements in the linked list and x is the value to be deleted. The second line contains n space-separated integers representing the linked list.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the elements of the linked list after deleting the first occurrence of value x. Elements are printed in order, separated by a single space. Print a newline after each test case. If the list becomes empty, print an empty line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The node with value 3 (the third element) is removed; the remaining list is printed.
The head node (value 1) is deleted; the new head is 2.
Value 4 is not present, so the list stays unchanged.

solution.approach: For each test case we build a singly linked list from the given values. To delete a node with value x we handle three cases: (1) the list is empty – nothing to do; (2) the head contains x – move the head pointer to the next node; (3) otherwise we traverse the list keeping a pointer to the current node and check its next node. When the next node holds x we bypass it by linking current->next to current->next->next and free the removed node. The traversal stops after the first deletion because only the first occurrence must be removed. Finally we traverse the possibly updated list and print its values. The algorithm is O(n) per test case in time and O(1) extra space (apart from the list nodes).
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) per test case, O(total N) for the whole input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the linked list of each test case; auxiliary space O(1).
